label: ai
topic: Identity shaped by relationships with women — sister, exchange students, a refugee friend
text:
If I trace the shape of who I am, I find it in the women who stood beside me, ahead of me, and sometimes quietly behind me.

My younger sister taught me that strength can be loud. She moves through the world with a kind of fearless honesty I once found intimidating. I remember watching her speak up in situations where I would have stayed silent, her voice steady and unapologetic. At first, I worried for her—worried that the world wouldn't always be kind to that kind of openness. But over time, I realized that her willingness to take up space wasn't something to fear; it was something to learn from.

In high school, I met exchange students who carried entire worlds within them. One of them arrived with a single suitcase and stories of a home I could barely imagine. She spoke about leaving behind everything familiar—language, culture, family—and starting over in a place where even simple interactions required effort. Yet she approached each day with curiosity instead of fear. From her, I learned that bravery isn't always dramatic; sometimes it's choosing to engage with the unknown, again and again.

Then there was my friend, a refugee, who understood loss in a way I had never experienced. She spoke about her past with a quiet clarity, never minimizing the difficulty but never allowing it to define her entirely. I remember sitting with her one afternoon as she described rebuilding her life piece by piece. There was no self-pity in her voice—only determination. From her, I learned that vulnerability and resilience are not opposites; they can exist side by side.

Each of these women showed me a different version of strength.

For a long time, I thought I had to choose between them—that I had to be either outspoken or reserved, fearless or cautious, independent or connected. But watching them, learning from them, I began to understand that identity isn't about choosing one path. It's about weaving together the lessons you've been given.

I've started to speak up more, carrying a bit of my sister's boldness with me. I've embraced new experiences with the curiosity of those exchange students, allowing myself to step outside my comfort zone. And I've learned to sit with my own vulnerabilities, understanding, as my friend showed me, that they are not weaknesses but sources of connection.

These women didn't just influence who I am—they expanded my understanding of who I could be.

Their stories live in the choices I make, in the way I navigate challenges, in the way I show up for others. And as I continue to grow, I carry their lessons with me, not as separate pieces, but as a collective foundation.

Who I am is not a single story. It is a reflection of the women who shaped me—and the courage they taught me to claim as my own.
